explanation:
Mathematics is a universal tool: algebra, geometry, calculus, and statistics are applied daily in engineering, science, finance, and everyday life. Trigonometric functions describe relationships between angles and sides in triangles and model periodic phenomena.

## How It's Calculated

This calculator applies trigonometric formulas to your input values, checking typical ranges and alerting you if values fall outside reasonable limits.

## Important Notes

This calculation is a reference guide. For critical decisions—whether in engineering, physics, or science—consult with a qualified professional. Values are current as of 2026 and reviewed regularly.

faq.q: What's the difference between radians and degrees?
faq.a: Degrees divide a circle into 360 parts, while radians measure angles using the radius. Both describe the same angle in different units. Convert using: radians = degrees × π/180.
Tangent is undefined when cosine equals zero, which occurs at 90°, 270°, 450°, and other odd multiples of 90°. At these points, the tangent function has a vertical asymptote.

faq.q: What is the Pythagorean trigonometric identity?
faq.a: The fundamental identity is sin²θ + cos²θ = 1. This relationship holds for any angle and is essential in trigonometry.

faq.q: How do I find an angle from its sine value?
faq.a: Use the inverse sine function, called arcsine (sin⁻¹ or arcsin). It returns the angle that produces a given sine value.

faq.q: What are real-world applications of trigonometric functions?
faq.a: Trigonometry is used in engineering, physics, navigation, surveying, and signal processing. Common applications include vectors, waves, oscillations, and projectile motion.

faq.q: Is this calculator free to use?
faq.a: Yes. All our calculators are completely free with no registration required.
We use internationally validated standard formulas. Results are reliable for reference and learning. For critical applications, consult with a professional.

faq.q: Do you store my data or calculations?
faq.a: No. All calculations happen in your browser. We don't send data to our servers or store your calculations.
